classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: "And on the morrow, when Agrippa was come, and Bernice with great pomp, and was entered into the place of hearing, with the chief captains, and principal men of the city, at Festus’ commandment Paul was brought forth.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See what an audience is gathered together for Paul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Having collected all his guards, the governor is come, and the king, and the tribunes, “with the principal men,” it says, “of the city.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then Paul being brought forth, see how he is proclaimed as conqueror.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Festus himself acquits him from the charges, for what says Festus?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Festus said, King Agrippa, and all men which are here present with us, ye see this man, about whom all the multitude of the Jews have dealt with me, both at Jerusalem, and also here, crying that he ought not to live any longer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when I found that he had committed nothing worthy of death, and that he himself hath appealed to Augustus, I have determined to send him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of whom I have no certain thing to write unto my lord.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore I have brought him forth before you, and especially before thee, O king Agrippa, that, after examination had, I might have somewhat to write.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it seemeth to me unreasonable to send a prisoner, and not withal to signify the crimes laid against him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how he accuses them, while he acquits him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: O what an abundance of justifications!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: After all these repeated examinations, the governor finds not how he may condemn him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They said he was worthy of death.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On this account he said also: “When I found,” says he “that he had committed nothing worthy of death.—Of whom I have no certain thing to write to my lord.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This too is a proof of Paul’s spotlessness, that the judge found nothing to say concerning him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Therefore I have brought him forth,” he says, “before you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it seemeth to me unreasonable to send a prisoner, and not withal to signify the crime laid against him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such were the great straits into which the Jews brought themselves and their rulers!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Agrippa said to Paul, Thou art permitted to speak for thyself.” (ch. xxvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From his great desire to hear, the king permits him to speak.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Paul speaks out forthwith with boldness, not flattering, but for this reason saying that he is happy, namely, because (Agrippa) knew all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then Paul stretched forth the hand, and answered for himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I think myself happy, king Agrippa, because I shall answer for myself this day before thee touching all the things whereof I am accused of the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Especially because I know thee to be expert in all questions which are among the Jews: wherefore I beseech thee to hear me patiently.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet, had he been conscious of guilt, he should have feared at being tried in the presence of one who knew all the facts: but this is a mark of a clear conscience, not to shrink from a judge who has an accurate knowledge of the circumstances, but even to rejoice, and to call himself happy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I beseech thee,” he says, “to hear me patiently.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since he is about to lengthen out his speech, and to say something about himself, on this account, he premises an entreaty, and (then) says: “My manner of life from my youth, which was at the first among mine own nation at Jerusalem, know all the Jews: which knew me from the beginning, if they would testify, that after the most straitest sect of our religion I lived a Pharisee.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then how should I have become a seditious person, who when young was (thus) testified of by all?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then too from his sect: “after the most straitest sect” says he, “of our religion I lived.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What then, if though the sect indeed be worthy of admiration, thou art evil?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Touching this also I call all to witness—touching my life and conversation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And now I stand and am judged for the hope of the promise made of God unto our fathers: unto which promise our twelve tribes, instantly serving God day and night, hope to come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For which hope’s sake, king Agrippa, I am accused of the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why should it be thought a thing incredible with you, that God should raise the dead?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Two arguments he lays down for the Resurrection: one, the argument from the prophets: and he does not bring forward any prophet (in particular,) but the doctrine itself as held by the Jews: the other and stronger one, the argument from the facts—(especially from this,) that Christ Himself held discourse with him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he lays the ground for this by (other) arguments, relating accurately his former madness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then too, with high commendation of the Jews, he says, “Night and day,” says he, “serving (God) look to attain unto.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that even if I had not been of unblemished life, it is not for this (doctrine) that I ought to be brought to trial:—“for which hope, king Agrippa, I am accused of the Jews.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And then another argument “Why should it be thought a thing incredible with you, that God should raise the dead?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since, if such an opinion had not existed, if they had not been brought up in these dogmas, but they were now for the first time brought in, perhaps some one might not have received the saying.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he tells, how he persecuted: this also helps the proof: and he brings forward the chief priests as witnesses, and the “strange cities,” and that he heard Him saying to him, “It is hard for thee to kick against the pricks,” and shows the mercifulness of God, that, though being persecuted He appeared (to men), and did that benefit not to me only, but also sent me as teacher to others: and shows also the prophecy, now come to pass, which he then heard, “Delivering thee from the people, and from the Gentiles, unto whom I send thee.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Showing all this, he says: “I verily thought with myself, that I ought to do many things contrary to the name of Jesus of Nazareth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Which thing I also did in Jerusalem: and many of the saints did I shut up in prison, having received authority from the chief priests; and when they were put to death, I gave my voice against them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And I punished them oft in every synagogue, and compelled them to blaspheme; and being exceedingly mad against them, I persecuted them even unto strange cities.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whereupon as I went to Damascus with authority and commission from the chief priests, at midday, O king, I saw in the way a light from heaven, above the brightness of the sun, shining round about me and them which journeyed with me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when we were all fallen to the earth, I heard a voice speaking unto me, and saying in the Hebrew tongue, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou Me? it is hard for thee to kick against the pricks.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And I said, Who art Thou, Lord?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he said, I am Jesus whom thou persecutest; but rise, and stand upon thy feet: for I have appeared unto thee for this purpose, to make thee a minister and a witness both of these things which thou hast seen, and of those things in the which I will appear unto thee: delivering thee from the people, and from the Gentiles, unto whom now I send thee, to open their eyes, and to turn them from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan unto God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 9–18):—observe how mildly he discourses—God, he says, said (this) to me, “that they may receive forgiveness of sins, and inheritance among them which are sanctified by faith that is in Me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By these things, says he, I was persuaded, by this vision He drew me to Himself, and so persuaded me, that I made no delay.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Whereupon, O king Agrippa, I was not disobedient unto the heavenly vision: but showed first unto them of Damascus, and at Jerusalem, and throughout all the coasts of Judea, and then to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God, and do works meet for repentance.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I therefore, who instructed others also concerning the most excellent way of living, how should I myself have become the author of sedition and contention?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For these causes the Jews caught me in the temple, and went about to kill me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Having therefore obtained help of God, I continue unto this day, witnessing both to small and great, saying none other things than those which the prophets and Moses did say should come.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how free from flattery his speech is, and how he ascribes the whole to God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then his boldness—but neither do I now desist: and the sure grounds—for it is from the prophets that I urge the question, “Whether the Christ was to suffer:” then the Resurrection and the promise, “Whether He, as the first to rise from the dead, should show light unto the people and to the Gentiles.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Festus saw the boldness, and what says he?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For Paul was all along addressing himself to the king—he was in a manner annoyed, and says to him, “Thou art beside thyself, Paul:” for, “while he thus discoursed, Festus said with a loud voice, Paul, thou art beside thyself: much learning doth make thee mad.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then says Paul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: With gentleness, “I am not mad,” says he, “most noble Festus; but speak forth the words of truth and soberness.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then too he gives him to understand why, turning from him, he addressed his speech to the king: “For the king knoweth of these things, before whom also I speak freely: for I am persuaded that none of these things are hidden from him: for this thing was not done in a corner.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows, that (the king) knows all perfectly; at the same time, all but saying to the Jews, And ye indeed ought to have known these things—for this is the meaning of that which he adds, “For this thing was not done in a corner.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Agrippa, said to Paul, ᾽Εν ὀλίγῳ thou persuadest me to be a Christian.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is ἐν ὀλίγῳ?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Within a little, παρὰ μικρόν.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Paul said, I could pray to God,” καὶ ἐν ὀλίγῳ καὶ ἐν πολλᾥ, (that is) “I could pray to God,” for my part, not “in little” (but “in much”): he does not simply pray, he prays (not briefly, but) with largeness—“that not only thou, but also all that hear me this day, were such as I am.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he adds, “except these bonds;” and yet it was matter of glory; true, but looking to their notion of it, therefore says he, “except these bonds.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And on the morrow,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Jews desisted ever since Paul exercised his right of appeal.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then also for him the theatre becomes a splendid one: “with great pomp” they were present.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Festus said,” etc. “The whole multitude of the Jews—“not some of them only, and others not so—“both at Jerusalem, and also here,” they said “that he ought not to live any longer.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And I having found,” etc. It shows that he did right in appealing to Cæsar.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if though they had no great matter to allege against him, yet those (at Jerusalem) were mad against him, with good reason may he go to Cæsar.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “That after examination had by you,” he says, “I may get somewhat to write.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how the matter is repeatedly put to the test.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Jews therefore may thank themselves for this vindication (of Paul), which would come to the ears of those also who were at Rome.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how they become the unwilling heralds both of their own wickedness and of Paul’s virtue, even to the emperor himself: so that Paul was carried away (to Rome) with more renown than if he had gone thither without bonds: for not as an impostor and a deceiver, after so many judges had acquitted him, was he now carried thither.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Quit therefore of all charges, among those with whom he was bred and born, and not only so, (but) thus free from all suspicion, he makes his appearance at Rome.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then Paul,” etc. (ch. xxvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he said not, Why is this? once for all I have appealed to Cæsar: I have been tried many times: when will there be an end of this? but what did he?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again he is ready to render an account, and that, before the man who was the best informed on the subject; and with much boldness, seeing they were not his judges to condemn him: but still, though they were not his judges, since that declaration was in force, “Unto Cæsar shalt thou go, he renders an account and gives full answers, “touching all the things,” and not merely on one and another here and there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They accuse me of sedition, accuse me of heresy, accuse me that I have profaned the temple: “touching all these things I answer for myself:” now that these are not things in accordance with my ways, my accusers themselves are witnesses: “my manner of life from my youth,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 4.) which is what he says on a former occasion “Being a zealot.” (ch. xxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when the whole people was present, then he challenges their testimony: not before the tribunal, but before Lysias, and again here, when more were present: whereas in that hearing there needed not much vindication of himself, since Lysias’ letter exculpated him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Know all the Jews,” he says, “which knew me from the beginning.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say what kind of life his was, but leaves it to their own conscience, and lays the whole stress on his sect, as he would not have chosen that sect, if he had been a man of evil disposition and bad character (πονηρὸς καὶ μοχθηρός).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But, for this hope” (mss. and Edd. αἱρέσεως) he says, “I stand and am judged.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This hope is honored among themselves also, because of this they pray, because of this they worship, that unto this they may attain: this same do I show forth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then, it is acting like madmen, to be doing all things for the sake of attaining to this, and yet to persecute him who believes in the same.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I indeed thought with myself,” that is, I determined, “to do many things contrary to the name of Jesus of Nazareth.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I was not one of Christ’s disciples: among those who fought against Him, was I.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence also he is a witness who has a right to be believed, because he, a man who was doing numberless things, makes war on the believers, persuading them to blaspheme, stirring up all against them, cities, rulers, and by himself doing all this of his own accord, was thus suddenly changed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then again the witnesses, those who were with him: next he shows what just cause he had to be persuaded, both from the light, and from the prophets, and from the results, and from the things which have now taken place.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See accordingly, how both from the prophets, and from these particulars, he confirms the proof to them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that he may not seem to be broaching some novelty, although he had great things to say, yet he again takes refuge with the prophets, and puts this as a question for discussion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now this had a stronger claim upon belief, as having actually come to pass: but since he alone saw (Christ), he again fetches proof of it from the prophets.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And see how he does not discourse alike in the court of justice, and in the assembly (of his own people); there indeed he says, “ye slew Him:” but here no such thing, that he might not kindle their anger more: but he shows the same thing, by saying, “Whether the Christ was to suffer.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He so frees them from accusations: for the prophets, he says, say this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore receive ye also the rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since he has mentioned the vision, he then without fear goes on to speak also of the good wrought by it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “To turn them from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan unto God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For to this end have I appeared unto thee” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 16–18), not to punish, but to make thee an Apostle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows the evils which possess unbelievers, “Satan, darkness;” the good things belonging to believers, light, God, “the inheritance of the saints.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whereupon, O king Agrippa,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He not only exhorts them to repent, but also to show forth a life worthy of admiration.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And see how everywhere the Gentiles are admitted into connection with the people (Israel): for those who were present were of the Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Testifying,” he says, “both to great and small,” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22) that is, both to distinguished and undistinguished.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is also for the soldiers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe: having left the post of defendant, he took up that of teacher—and therefore also it is that Festus says to him, “Thou art beside thyself”—but then, that he may not seem to be himself the teacher, he brings in the prophets, and Moses: “Whether the Christ was to suffer, whether He as the first to rise from the dead should show light both to the people, and to the Gentiles.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Festus said with a loud voice”—in such anger and displeasure (did he speak)—“Paul, thou art beside thyself.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then said Paul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I am not mad,” etc. “For this thing,” he says, “was not done in a corner.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here he speaks of the Cross, of the Resurrection: that the doctrine was come to every part of the world.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “King Agrippa,” he says, “believest thou”—he does not say, the Resurrection, but—“the prophets?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he forestalls him, and says: “I know that thou believest.” ᾽Εν ὀλίγῳ (i.e. within a little,) “almost thou persuadeth the to be a Christian.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Paul did not understand what the phrase ἐν ὀλίγῳ meant: he thought it meant ἐξ ὀλιγου (i.e. with little cost or trouble), wherefore also he answers (as) to this: so unlearned was he.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he said not, I do not wish (that), but, “I pray that not only thou, but also all that hear.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how free from flattery his speech is.—“I pray that this day they may be all such as I am, except these bonds.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He, the man that glories in his bonds, that puts them forth as a golden chain, deprecates them for these men: for they were as yet too weak in their minds, and it was rather in condescension that he so spake.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what could be better than those bonds which always in his Epistles he prefers (to all things else), saying, “Paul, a prisoner of Jesus Christ:” (Eph. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 1) and again, “On this account I am bound with this chain” (Acts xxviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 20), “but the word of God is not bound;” and, “Even unto bonds, as an evil-doer.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The punishment was twofold.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if indeed he had been so bound, as with a view to his good, the thing would have carried with it some consolation: but now (he is bound) both “as an evil-doer,” and as with a view to very ill consequences; yet for none of these things cared he.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such is a soul winged with heavenly love.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if those who cherish the foul (earthly passion which men call) love, think nothing either glorious of precious, but those things alone which tend to gratify their lust, they think both glorious and honorable, and their mistress is everything to them; much more do those, who have been taken captive by this heavenly love, think nothing of the cost (τὰ ἐπιτίμια).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if we do not understand what I am saying, it is no marvel, while we are unskilled in this Divine Wisdom.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if any one be caught with the fire of Christ’s love, he becomes such as a man would become who dwelt alone upon the earth, so utterly careless is he for glory or disgrace: but just as if he dwelt alone, he would care for nothing, no more does he in this case.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As for trials, he so despises them, both scourges and imprisonments, as though the body in which he suffers these things were another’s and not his own, or as though he had got a body made of adamant: while as for the sweet things of this life, he so laughs them to scorn, is so insensible to them, as we are insensible of dead bodies, being ourselves dead.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is as far from being taken captive by any passion, as the gold refined in the fire and purified is free from alloy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For even as flies would not dart into the midst of a flame, but fly from it, so the passions dare not even to come near this man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would that I could bring forward examples of all this from among ourselves: but since we are at a loss for such, we must needs betake ourselves to this same Paul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe him then, how he felt towards the whole world.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The world is crucified unto me,” he says, “and I unto the world” (Gal. vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 14): I am dead to the world, and the world is dead to me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again: “It is no longer I that live, but Christ liveth in me.” (ib.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, to show you that he was as it were in solitude, and so looked upon the things present, hear himself saying, “While we look not at the things which are seen, but at the things which are not seen.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What sayest thou?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet what thou sayest is the contrary; thou seest the things invisible, and the visible thou seest not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such eyes as thou hadst gotten, such are the eyes which are given by Christ: for as these bodily eyes see indeed the things that are seen, but things unseen they see not: so those (heavenly eyes) do the contrary: none that beholds the invisible things, beholds the visible: no one beholding the things seen, beholds the invisible.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or is not this the case with us also?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when having turned our mind inwards we think of any of the unseen things, our views become raised above the things on earth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us despise glory: let us be willing to be laughed at rather than to be praised.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he indeed who is laughed at is nothing hurt: but he who is praised is much hurt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not think much of those things which terrify men, but as we do in the case of children, this let us do here: namely, if we see any one terrifying children, we do not hold that man in admiration: since in fact whoever does frighten, only frightens children; for were it a man, he could not frighten him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just as those who frighten (children in sport), do this either by drawing up their eyelids, or by otherwise distorting their face, but with the eye looking naturally and mild they would not be able to do this: so these others do this, by distorting their mental vision (τὸ διορατικὸν τἥς διανοίας).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that of a mild man and beautiful in soul nobody would be afraid; on the contrary, we all respect him, honor and venerate him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See ye not, how the man who causes terror is also an object of hatred and abhorrence to us all?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For of those things which are only able to terrify what do we not turn away from?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is it not so with wild beasts, with sounds, with sights, with places, with the air, such as darkness?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not therefore think it a great thing, if men fear us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, in the first place, no man indeed is frightened at us: and, secondly, it is no great thing (if they were).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Virtue is a great good: and see how great.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: However wretched we may deem the things by means of which it consists, yet we admire virtue itself, and count them blessed (that have it).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For who would not count the patient sufferer blessed, although poverty and such like things seem to be wretched?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When therefore it shines forth through those things which seem to be wretched, see how surpassingly great this is!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thinkest thou much, O man, because thou art in power?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what sort of power? say, was it conferred by appointment?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (If so,) of men thou hast received power: appoint thyself to it from within.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the ruler is not he who is so called, but he who is really so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as a king could not make a physician or an orator, so neither can he make a ruler: since it is not the (imperial) letters nor the name that makes a ruler.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, if you will, let any man build a medicine-shop, let him also have pupils, let him have instruments too and drugs, and let him visit those who are sick: are these things sufficient to make a physician?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By no means: but there is need of art, and without that, not only do these things profit nothing, but they even hurt: since it were better that he who is not a physician should not even possess medicines.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He that possesses them not, neither saves nor destroys: but he that possesses them, destroys, if he knows not how to use them: since the healing power is not only in the nature of the medicines, but also in the art of the person applying them: where this is not, all is marred.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such also is the ruler: he has for instruments, his voice, anger, executioners, banishments, honors, gifts, and praises; he has also for medicines, the law; has also for his patients, men; for a place to practise in, the court of justice; for pupils, he has the soldiers: if then he know not the science of healing, all these profit him nothing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The judge is a physician of souls, not of bodies: but if this art of healing the bodies needs so much care, much more that of healing the soul, since the soul is of more importance than the body.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then not the mere having the name of ruler is to be a ruler: since others also are called by great names: as Paul, Peter, James, and John: but the names do not make them that which they are called, as neither does my name make me (to be that which John was); I bear indeed the same name with that blessed man, but I am not the same thing (ὁμώνυμος, οὐ μὴν συνώνυμος), I am not John, but am called so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the same way they are not rulers, but are called so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But those others are rulers even without these adjuncts, just as also a physician, though he may not actually practise his science, yet if he have it in his soul, he is a physician.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Those are rulers, who bear rule over themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there are these four things, soul, family; city, world: and the things form a regular progression (ὁδᾥ προβαίνει).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He therefore that is to superintend a family, and order it well, must first bring his own soul into order; for it is his family: but if he cannot order his own family, where there is but one soul, where he himself is master, where he is always along with himself, how shall he order others?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He that is able to regulate his own soul, and makes the one part to rule, the other to be subject, this man will be able to regulate a family also: but he that can do this by a family, can do it by a city also: and if by a city, then also by the world.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if he cannot do this for his own soul, how then shall he be able to do it for the world?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These things have been spoken by me, that we may not be excited about offices of rule; that we may know what ruling is: for this (which is so called) is not ruling, but a there object of derision, mere slavery, and many other names one might call it by.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Tell me, what is proper to a ruler? is it not to help one’s subjects, and to do them good?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then, if this be not the case? how shall he help others, who has not helped himself? he who has numberless tyrannies of the passions in his own soul, how shall he root out those of others?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, with respect to “luxury” or delightful living: the true luxury or delight is not this (which is so called), but quite another thing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as we have shown that the ruler is not he who is so called, but another (who has something more than the name), so the person who lives indeed in delight is another sort of person (than he whom we so describe).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For “luxury” or delightful living seems indeed to be, the enjoying pleasure and the gratifying the belly: yet it is not this thing, but the contrary: it is, to have a soul worthy of admiration, and to be in a state of pleasure.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For let there be a man eating, drinking, and wantoning; then let him suffer cares and loss of spirits: can this man be said to be in a state of delight?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore, it is not eating and drinking, it is the being in pleasure, that makes true luxury or delightful living.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let there be a man who gets only dry bread, and let him be filled with gladness: is not this pleasure?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well then, it is the true luxury.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us see then, to whom this befalls—whether to the rich, or to those who are not rich?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Neither to the one part altogether, nor to the other, but to those who so order their own souls, that they may not have many grounds for sorrows.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And where is such a life as this to be found? for I see you all eager and wishing to hear what this life is which has no sorrows.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well then, let this be acknowledged first by you, that this is pleasure, this the true luxury, to have no sorrow to cause annoyance; and ask not of me meats, and wine, and sauces, and silken robes, and a sumptuous table.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if I shall show that apart from all these such a life as that is present (within our reach), then welcome thou this pleasure, and this life: for the most part of painful things happen to us from our not calculating things as we ought.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who then will have the most sorrows—he that cares for none of these things, or he that cares for them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He that fears changes, or he that does not fear?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He that is in dread of jealousy, of envy, of false accusations, of plottings, of destruction, or he that stands aloof from these fears?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He that wants many things, or he that wants nothing?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He that is a slave to masters without number, or he that is a slave to none?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He that has need of many things, or he that is free?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He that has one lord to fear, or he that fears despots innumerable?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well then, greater is the pleasure here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This then let us pursue, and not be excited about the things present: but let us laugh to scorn all the pomp of life, and everywhere practise moderation, that we may be enabled so to pass through this life, that it may be without pain, and to attain unto the good things promised, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost together be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
